methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $15M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
1872, a Seed-stage robotics and manufacturing company founded by Dan Summers, has raised $15M to date. The valuation analysis draws from a sector comp universe of 9 private robotics firms and 1 public comparable, though peer-level data constraints limit triangulation options.

Stage-progression valuation represents the only applicable method given the sparse narrow peer set. Starting from the $15M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a typical 2.5x multiplier for progression from Seed to the next funding stage yields $38M. The peer-median approach was not viable; while 7 robotics peers exist broadly, none met the filters for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier comparability required for meaningful cross-sectional analysis. Relying on all-sector medians would introduce excessive dispersion risk.

The triangulation supports a valuation range of $32M (low) to $43M (high), with a median estimate of $38M. This single-method output carries material uncertainty, particularly around timing to next funding event and the applicability of the 2.5x stage multiplier to 1872's specific trajectory. The lack of narrow comparable data in Robotics & Manufacturing at the Seed stage represents the primary limitation. Resolution of this range depends on future financials, capital deployment metrics, and closer peer benchmarking as the company progresses toward Series A.
